comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a physical implementation of Conway's Game of Life, with commenters sharing personal experiences, alternative approaches, and reflections on the project's cost and appeal. Some express enthusiasm for the concept and potential future technologies, while others critique the physicality and cost-effectiveness of the build.

comments.author: Nevermark
comments.text:
This is fantastic.

I happen to own a physical pong table. Everyone loves it. [0] There is something magical about material and only-does-one-thing instantiations of digital things.

——

I am really looking forward to a flat table top screen consisting of millions of magnetically-controlled vertical-extruding rod pixels, with actual color controlled pixels along their length. For an amazing table display/extrusion of D&D dungeons and terrain, for miniatures.

A little sensor ability, and when the extrusion "scrolls" laterally, it could create little lips that move the miniatures too, so they stay "in place". And move NPCs around at the DMs direction. Now turn of the room lights, and use the light pixels not just for color, but for lighting effects like wall lamps flickering, add spooky position-distributed sounds...

That has to be coming soon, right?

Some part of me wants to describe it Claude, iterate, and say "Now polish that design, economically optimize the parts, link in the supply chain, and ship me the first review unit." That has to be coming soon, right?

The lines separating planes of reality, they are a blurring, quickly.

——

[0] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=84Ymt9BAq5s
comments.analysis: This comment provides a thoughtful and imaginative perspective on physical digital displays, linking personal experience with future technological possibilities, reflecting a deep understanding of the topic.

comments.author: alnwlsn
comments.text:
Wow, in switches alone this would cost me $1200! Which actually isn't that bad for a one off-like this with easily available components - I've seen worse. You can of course take your chances with Aliexpress Specials but be warned: some of those cheap switches are cheap switches (sometimes, they're barely even switches). And you won't be getting them inside of 2 weeks.

I think I would have tried mechanical keyboarding switches that can take an LED and a clear keycap. Not really the same effect, but you could probably have gotten out to 24x24 for the same price.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a realistic cost assessment and practical advice about component quality and sourcing, adding valuable context for those considering similar projects.

comments.author: kangalioo
comments.text: A much cheaper way might have been to buy a couple of Novation Launchpads. 8x8 full RGB tactile buttons for 90€, MIDI-controllable. Four of those next to another for 16x16 at 360€ plus a little bit for cables and controller comes out at 1/3 the price
comments.analysis: This comment provides a practical and cost-effective alternative for building a similar project, demonstrating knowledge of available hardware options.

comments.author: kanapala
comments.text: Technology Will Save Us, from a brighter past, produced the Arcade Coder as their swan song. It had 12×12 tactile buttons with color leds inside, a companion app to program it with scratch like code blocks, and interactive tutorials. It's ESP32 based. There's an effort to continue it's life, ast the app was on the app store for a brief time. https://github.com/padraigfl/twsu-arcade-coder-esp32
comments.analysis: This comment shares relevant historical and technical information about a similar device, enriching the discussion with concrete examples.

comments.author: eps
comments.text: I saw one in a computer museum in Switzerland. It was a much larger field, it was just large orange LEDs (or were they tubes?), but it also cycled between a dozen of different cell automata games. Something about being able to see individual "pixels" made it really mesmerizing.
comments.analysis: The comment provides an anecdotal account of a similar installation, adding experiential insight into the appeal of physical cell automata displays.

comments.author: self_awareness
comments.text: That's not a "physical" version of game of life -- that's a digital version, like every version, but with bigger pixels.
comments.analysis: This comment critically clarifies the nature of the project, distinguishing between truly physical and digital implementations, which is an important conceptual distinction.

comments.author: alexb_
comments.text: This isn't what this is about, but I wonder if anyone has created a fully mechanized version of Conway's Game of Life. The fact that you only have to follow 4 rules, and each cell only needs to observe its neighbors to follow those rules, makes me wonder if it's possible to track these things mechanically. I unfortunately don't know enough about mechanical engineering to do such a thing.
comments.analysis: The comment raises an interesting engineering question about mechanical implementations, showing curiosity and understanding of the game's rules, though lacking concrete examples.

comments.author: slow_typist
comments.text:
Très cool.

A grid of capacitive touch sensors could be printed directly on the pcb, bringing down costs by a degree of magnitude. Real switches are much more satisfying though.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a practical suggestion for reducing costs while acknowledging the tactile experience, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: jFriedensreich
comments.text: how is this real life more than a monitor, its just bigger pixels. i was expecting programmable live cells or something mechanical
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a common misconception or disappointment about the project, highlighting the difference between digital displays and truly physical automata.

comments.author: meindnoch
comments.text: Eh. I thought they had somehow created a physical realization of Conway's Game of Life.
comments.analysis: Similar to the previous comment, this reflects a misunderstanding or unmet expectation regarding the physicality of the project.
